nativeRange.habitat: The Pygmy Hatchetfish inhabits small, shaded, shallow woodland streams and forest creeks with slow-moving, heavily vegetated blackwater. In the wild, the fish spends its time at or just below the surface of these streams, which typically have leaf-litter substrates and receive little direct sunlight.

decorPreferences: floating plants (Amazon frogbit, salvinia) to dim surface light
overhanging riparian vegetation or surface cover
driftwood and leaf litter for tannin-stained water
tight-fitting cover glass — mandatory (extreme escape risk)

diet.notes: The Pygmy Hatchetfish is a strict surface feeder in the wild, taking small invertebrates and fallen insects from the water's surface. In the aquarium, it generally accepts floating micro-pellets, fine flake, freeze-dried daphnia, and small freeze-dried bloodworms. Live or frozen wingless fruit flies (Drosophila) and micro-worms are highly recommended as regular enrichment. Food must be presented at or on the surface — this species will not readily feed from mid-water or the bottom, and fish kept in tanks without surface-floating food may gradually decline.

goodWith: small tetras (ember tetra, neon tetra, cardinal tetra)
avoidWith: any fish large enough to swallow them
boisterous or fast-moving mid-water schoolers that cause startling

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by 'opencage' (798x800). Sharp, clean two-fish view on white background clearly showing the deep hatchet-shaped keeled belly and dark lateral stripe of C. myersi; small corner watermark only.

Photo by Carnat Joel (1024x777). In-situ group/school shot showing several individuals and hatchet body profile; good for representing this schooling species, but carries a text caption banner and frame overlay plus edge watermark.

summary: The Pygmy Hatchetfish is the smallest member of the hatchetfish family, reaching only about 2.5 cm. A strict surface dweller from Amazonian forest streams, it requires soft, acidic water and a tight-fitting lid — it will launch itself through any gap. The species does best in schools of six or more alongside very small, peaceful tankmates.
careNotes: The Pygmy Hatchetfish is the smallest member of the family Gasteropelecidae and one of the smallest freshwater aquarium fish available in the hobby, reaching only about 2.0–2.5 cm. It originates from small, shaded woodland streams in the Peruvian Amazon basin near Yurimaguas, with a secondary population documented in Bolivia. These streams are slow-moving, heavily shaded, and carry very soft, acidic blackwater with extensive leaf litter and surface vegetation. Replicating these conditions — pH 4.0–7.0, gH 1–8, temperature 23–26°C, tannin-stained water, dim lighting with floating plant cover, and minimal surface agitation — is essential for long-term health and to minimise stress. A tight-fitting cover glass with no open slots is absolutely mandatory: hatchetfish are notorious jumpers and will launch themselves through the smallest gap when startled. All Carnegiella species glide just at or immediately below the water surface; the pygmy hatchetfish rarely if ever descends into mid-water. It must therefore be fed with floating foods only — micro-pellets, fine flakes, freeze-dried daphnia, and live or frozen wingless fruit flies. Underfeeding is a common cause of decline, as the fish will not compete with mid-water feeders. Keep in schools of at least 6 (ideally 8–12) as isolation causes chronic stress. Tankmates must be very small and peaceful; avoid any fish large enough to startle or consume them. Wild-caught specimens (most trade stock) benefit from prophylactic treatment for internal parasites and ich before introduction to the display tank. Less commonly traded than the Marbled Hatchetfish (C. strigata) or the Black-winged Hatchetfish (C. marthae) [fw-fish-153].
breedingNotes: Breeding has not been recorded in captivity. In the wild the species is presumed to scatter eggs among surface vegetation or floating leaf matter, as with related Carnegiella. No reliable aquarium breeding protocol exists; all trade specimens are wild-caught.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Carnegiella myersi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (20–25 mm SL), temperature (23–26°C), pH (4.0–7.0), hardness (18–108 ppm / ~1–6 dGH), minimum tank footprint (50×30 cm, ~45 L), diet (insectivorous surface feeder), grouping (school of 6+), native range (Peruvian Amazon near Yurimaguas; secondary Bolivia population), habitat (shaded shallow woodland streams), and breeding (not recorded in captivity).

additional.name: FishBase — Carnegiella myersi
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 2.2 cm SL), temperature (23–26°C), pH (5.5–6.5), hardness (dH up to 20), taxonomy (Gasteropelecidae, Characiformes), native range (Amazon basin, Peru), conservation status (LC, IUCN 2020). FishBase pH lower bound of 5.5 is narrower than SeriouslyFish 4.0; used SeriouslyFish broader range as primary for aquarium guidance.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Hatchetfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General hatchetfish guide noting pygmy hatchetfish as smallest species (1–2.5 cm), temperature 24–27°C, minimum 20-gallon (~75 L) tank for hatchetfish generally, school of 6–12 required, critical escape-risk warning (will jump through narrowest gap), surface-feeding behaviour, and suitable community tankmates. Minimum tank volume of 45 L used per SeriouslyFish species-specific guidance; 75 L used as a practical community tank recommendation.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding has been recorded for this species. All specimens available in the aquarium trade are wild-collected. In the wild, the Pygmy Hatchetfish is presumed to scatter eggs among surface vegetation or floating leaf matter, as with other members of the genus Carnegiella.
